headlines.title: NEW CROP PEACHES AND CANTELOUPES ON FORT WORTH MARKET
headlines.subtitle: Peaches Sell Around $3 Per Crate and Cantaloupes at $3 Per Dozen.
headlines.fullText: The first canteloupes and peaches es the season were received by local com- mission houses Wednesday. The peaches are of poor quality, but are up to the average of this time of the year, and sell at $3 per four-banker crate. headlines.title: NEW YORK COTTON
headlines.subtitle: Texas Rains Precipitate Further
headlines.fullText: NEW YORK MIS Med AT- cessive rains over the western cotton belt and deficient moisture in the east gave renewed strength to the cotton market today. Futures opened & to 15 higher and ruled steady. Buying was general and prices ad- vanced further. Shorts were nervous and offers light in all options. scoreity of high-grade cotton gives strength to the May option, which is making sensational advances. Crop reports are by no means assur. ing. while continental demand is good.

headlines.title: MOSLAH TEMPLE TO BE INSTALLED ON JUNE 26
headlines.subtitle: Invitation to Preside at Cere- monies Extended to Imper- ial Potentate Smith.
headlines.fullText: Date for the installation and ceremo- nial of Mosiah Temple has been de- elded on for Friday, June 26. Details of the event have not yet been worked out, however, and Potentate E. J. Ho- say does not know yet who will inati- tute the temple. An invitation to do so has been ex- tended to Imperial Potentate Freder- ick Smith of Rochester, N. Y., but no reply has been received.

headlines.title: Y. M. B. L. WILL HEAR COMMITTEE REPORT
headlines.subtitle: Las Enough Signatures Obtained to Insure Vote on City Manager Plan, Claim.
headlines.fullText: A report of the committee on the 1.40 business manager plan of city govern- ment will be submitted at a meeting of the Young Men's Business League to be held tonight at 8 o'clock at the Enough signatures have been cured to insure the calling of an elec- tion to make the necessary charter change. A meeting of the directors will held at fels o'clock at the Selbold and it is possible some of the matters con- aldered at the luncheon which will be served then will be carfie over to the general meeting at the Chamber of Commerce

headlines.title: FONDREN CASE IS AFFIRMED BY HIGH COURT;ONEDISSENTS
headlines.fullText: The case of W. E. Fondren, com- victed in Tarrant county of being an miper. accomplice in an alleged criminal oper- ation and sentenced to five years in the penitentiary, was affirmed Wednesday by the court of criminal appeals, ne- cording to a dispatch from Austin. As- sociate Justice Davidson filed a dis- setting opinion, in which he insisted that the evidence was insufficient to show that Fondren was an accom- plice. Fondren, who was tried here the June of last year, lives at Adell, in Parker county, and is a prominent farmer, and banker of arms The girl in the case, Daisy Moore, was ser here to save was convicted and his case was af- firmed by the court of criminal ap- peata Feb. 18.

headlines.title: URRUTIA, OUTCAST, MAY BE U. S. CITIZEN
headlines.fullText: GALVESTON, May 27 .- Dr. Aure- lano Urrutia, former foreign minister Her from His rative ation et taking out citizenship pa- pers and becoming an American resi- dent. lie expressed belief in reports that Huerta had fled from Mexico City saying that the de facto president had no ether hope of saving his life. squally an outcast now from both Dr. Urrutia feels that he will never again be able to live in Mexico on ac- count of the violent hatred against him He declined to; discuss eventualities ir the Mexican situation

headlines.title: WEINERT NOT TO RUN FOR LIEUT. GOVERNOR
headlines.fullText: AUSTIN, May 27 .- The magnitude of afstate campaign and the expense con- nected with it Is given as a reason by Secretary of State Weinert for fo running for lieutenant governor. Weinert issued a statement night thanking the press and his friends for giving him consideration as worthy interests were such that he could m sacrifice them to run for office.
